APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses event factuality, a clear NLP problem of interest to top-tier NLP/ML venues and targets the right audience (semantic and information-extraction researchers). Its approach (two-step pipeline with attention-based CNN/BiLSTM over dependency paths), use of a standard dataset (FactBank), comparisons to established baselines, and standard metrics and significance testing align with the methodological and evaluation expectations of such conferences. Minor fit concerns include the absence of stated limitations and no mention of released code, which can affect reproducibility and community uptake but do not preclude venue relevance. | B(4): This paper fits well within a top NLP venue: it addresses event factuality identification, proposes a practical two-step pipeline with attention-based CNN and BiLSTM components, and evaluates on a standard dataset (FactBank) against sensible baselines with appropriate metrics and significance testing. From an applied-systems perspective the pipeline design and reported hyperparameters make it attractive for real-world adoption, though lack of mentioned code release and unstated limitations slightly reduces reproducibility and broader impact. Overall the work targets the right audience and uses expected methods/evaluations, meriting a strong but not perfect fit.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses event factuality, a clear NLP problem of interest to top-tier NLP/ML venues and targets the right audience (semantic and information-extraction researchers). Its approach (two-step pipeline with attention-based CNN/BiLSTM over dependency paths), use of a standard dataset (FactBank), comparisons to established baselines, and standard metrics and significance testing align with the methodological and evaluation expectations of such conferences. Minor fit concerns include the absence of stated limitations and no mention of released code, which can affect reproducibility and community uptake but do not preclude venue relevance.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper fits well within a top NLP venue: it addresses event factuality identification, proposes a practical two-step pipeline with attention-based CNN and BiLSTM components, and evaluates on a standard dataset (FactBank) against sensible baselines with appropriate metrics and significance testing. From an applied-systems perspective the pipeline design and reported hyperparameters make it attractive for real-world adoption, though lack of mentioned code release and unstated limitations slightly reduces reproducibility and broader impact. Overall the work targets the right audience and uses expected methods/evaluations, meriting a strong but not perfect fit.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles the meaningful task of event factuality identification and proposes a two-step pipeline with attention-based CNN/BiLSTM components evaluated on FactBank, so it offers pragmatic improvements that practitioners in this niche can use. However, the contribution is largely an engineering pipeline with black-box neural components and no stated analysis of error propagation between the two stages or probing/interpretability studies; these omissions limit its conceptual influence. Because code and theoretical guarantees are not mentioned and there is no articulation of broader implications beyond the subfield, the work is useful but unlikely to substantially change how researchers think about factuality modeling or open major new directions. | B(3): This paper tackles a useful practical problem—event factuality detection—with an end-to-end pipeline that extracts SIPs/sources and applies attention-enhanced BiLSTM+CNN models, which is valuable for applied systems that must work from raw text. The empirical evaluation on FactBank against rule-based and prior statistical baselines and reporting of hyperparameters supports reproducibility and suggests consistent gains that practitioners could adopt. However, its impact appears moderate rather than transformative: it refines task-specific engineering rather than introducing a radically new paradigm, and adoption may be limited by lack of mentioned code release and broader validation beyond FactBank. Overall, a solid, practically oriented contribution likely to be built upon within the subfield but not to redefine it.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the meaningful task of event factuality identification and proposes a two-step pipeline with attention-based CNN/BiLSTM components evaluated on FactBank, so it offers pragmatic improvements that practitioners in this niche can use. However, the contribution is largely an engineering pipeline with black-box neural components and no stated analysis of error propagation between the two stages or probing/interpretability studies; these omissions limit its conceptual influence. Because code and theoretical guarantees are not mentioned and there is no articulation of broader implications beyond the subfield, the work is useful but unlikely to substantially change how researchers think about factuality modeling or open major new directions.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles a useful practical problem—event factuality detection—with an end-to-end pipeline that extracts SIPs/sources and applies attention-enhanced BiLSTM+CNN models, which is valuable for applied systems that must work from raw text. The empirical evaluation on FactBank against rule-based and prior statistical baselines and reporting of hyperparameters supports reproducibility and suggests consistent gains that practitioners could adopt. However, its impact appears moderate rather than transformative: it refines task-specific engineering rather than introducing a radically new paradigm, and adoption may be limited by lack of mentioned code release and broader validation beyond FactBank. Overall, a solid, practically oriented contribution likely to be built upon within the subfield but not to redefine it.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): From the supplied summary the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early — the two-step supervised framework and attention-based models are explicit. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and well-defined, with hyperparameters reported and related work coverage adequate. Figures/tables/examples are not described in detail in the summary, so while presentation and writing quality are reported as good, a small deduction is warranted because some empirical presentation elements could be more informative or better highlighted for a reader seeking quick intuition. | B(4): From the provided summary the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (two-step pipeline, SIP detector, attention-based BiLSTM+CNN factuality model). Notation and hyperparameters are reported and appear consistent, and the experiments/tables (datasets, baselines, metrics) are clearly presented; related work coverage is adequate and overall writing quality is good. A minor presentation gap is the absence of an explicit limitations section and no mention of released code, which would help clarify scope and reproducibility for applied practitioners.

reviewer_a.justification: From the supplied summary the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early — the two-step supervised framework and attention-based models are explicit. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and well-defined, with hyperparameters reported and related work coverage adequate. Figures/tables/examples are not described in detail in the summary, so while presentation and writing quality are reported as good, a small deduction is warranted because some empirical presentation elements could be more informative or better highlighted for a reader seeking quick intuition.

reviewer_b.justification: From the provided summary the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (two-step pipeline, SIP detector, attention-based BiLSTM+CNN factuality model). Notation and hyperparameters are reported and appear consistent, and the experiments/tables (datasets, baselines, metrics) are clearly presented; related work coverage is adequate and overall writing quality is good. A minor presentation gap is the absence of an explicit limitations section and no mention of released code, which would help clarify scope and reproducibility for applied practitioners.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates on the standard FactBank dataset and compares against a classical rule-based system (Saurí 2008), a MaxEnt baselines (de Marneffe et al. 2012), and a CNN+CNN variant of the proposed model using appropriate metrics and statistical tests, which makes the experimental setup largely fair. However, the baselines are somewhat dated and the authors do not indicate whether any more recent state-of-the-art or stronger neural baselines were considered or outperform their method; while related work coverage is described as adequate, this omission prevents the comparisons from being fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares against sensible and task-relevant baselines (Saurí 2008 rules and de Marneffe et al. 2012 MaxEnt) and a strong model variant (CNN+CNN), all evaluated on the same FactBank data using standard precision/recall/F1 with macro/micro measures and significance testing, which supports fair comparisons. Hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is described as adequate, but the comparison appears to omit potentially more recent neural baselines and broader state-of-the-art models, so while comparisons are good they are not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates on the standard FactBank dataset and compares against a classical rule-based system (Saurí 2008), a MaxEnt baselines (de Marneffe et al. 2012), and a CNN+CNN variant of the proposed model using appropriate metrics and statistical tests, which makes the experimental setup largely fair. However, the baselines are somewhat dated and the authors do not indicate whether any more recent state-of-the-art or stronger neural baselines were considered or outperform their method; while related work coverage is described as adequate, this omission prevents the comparisons from being fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against sensible and task-relevant baselines (Saurí 2008 rules and de Marneffe et al. 2012 MaxEnt) and a strong model variant (CNN+CNN), all evaluated on the same FactBank data using standard precision/recall/F1 with macro/micro measures and significance testing, which supports fair comparisons. Hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is described as adequate, but the comparison appears to omit potentially more recent neural baselines and broader state-of-the-art models, so while comparisons are good they are not fully comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper’s empirical claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments on FactBank with reasonable baselines and statistical testing, evidencing overall experimental soundness. However, there is a consistent lack of deeper analyses (no ablation/probing to isolate component contributions, no investigation of error propagation between pipeline stages) and limited reproducibility/limitations discussion, which undermines confidence in the claimed mechanisms and leads to a modest downgrade.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and evaluates them on FactBank against reasonable baselines with statistical tests, so experimental support exists. However, there is no indication of formal/theoretical motivation or guarantees for the two-step pipeline or attention architectures, and the summary omits any analysis of error propagation between the SIP-extraction stage and the factuality classifier. Additionally, no targeted ablation studies or interpretability/probing analyses are reported to isolate each component’s contribution, which raises concerns about the sufficiency of evidence for the claimed model mechanisms.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly articulated and appear supported by experiments on FactBank with appropriate baselines (rules, MaxEnt, and a CNN variant), macro/micro F1 reporting, and statistical testing, which indicates a sound experimental methodology. There are no mathematical proofs required and none are missing; the architectural descriptions (BiLSTM over dependency paths + CNN with attention) are technically plausible and align with my preference for deployable hybrid models. Minor concerns remain about reproducibility (code not mentioned) and the absence of stated limitations or deeper error analysis, so I deduct one point for these practical gaps that don't invalidate the core technical claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper assembles established neural components (attention, CNNs, BiLSTMs) into a two-step pipeline and represents an engineering-oriented rather than theoretical advance. The work provides a useful, non-trivial applied contribution (novel instantiation of attention-based CNNs for SIP detection and dependency-path modeling) but lacks fundamental novelty or deep analysis, so a moderate originality score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies well-known neural components (attention, CNNs, BiLSTMs) in a two-step pipeline for event factuality and introduces an attention-based CNN for SIP detection—an engineering instantiation rather than a principled theoretical advance. The problem (event factuality) and use of dependency paths and auxiliary lexical features are well-studied, and the method largely combines existing architectures without formal motivation, guarantees, or analysis of error propagation across the pipeline. From a theory-purist perspective this is therefore an incremental contribution rather than a genuinely new idea.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a concrete two-step pipeline and novel application of attention-based CNNs for SIP detection plus an attention-enhanced BiLSTM+CNN architecture over shortest dependency paths, which differentiates it from prior rule-based and MaxEnt approaches on FactBank. However, the components (attention, BiLSTM, CNN over dependency paths, auxiliary lexical features) are established techniques, so the contribution appears as a non-trivial engineering combination and extension rather than a fundamentally new idea. From an applied-systems perspective this is useful and informative, but not highly groundbreaking to experts in event factuality or neural NLP.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation on FactBank is sound with appropriate baselines and metrics, but the paper lacks deeper analysis (limited ablations/sensitivity studies), discussion of failure cases/limitations, and code release which weakens reproducibility. Reviewer A’s stronger critique about missing theoretical motivation and error-propagation analysis is valid but not fatal given the solid applied results noted by Reviewer B. Given these strengths and the noted gaps, a middle-ground score reflects a practically useful contribution that needs more analysis and transparency to be fully convincing.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is empirical and compares against sensible baselines on FactBank with standard metrics, but it lacks the theoretical motivation or formal analysis I expect for a multi-stage model (no identifiability/guarantees). The paper also omits discussion or quantification of error propagation through the two-step pipeline and reports fewer than the three targeted ablations I require (only a CNN+CNN variant is mentioned), which weakens confidence in component-wise contributions. Related work coverage is adequate and hyperparameters are reported, but absence of stated limitations and no code availability further reduce reproducibility and transparency.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the experiments are solid: evaluation on FactBank with multiple baselines (rules, MaxEnt, and a CNN variant), standard metrics (precision/recall/F1, macro/micro) and significance testing support the core claims about the two-step pipeline and attention-based models. However, the paper appears to lack explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses beyond the CNN+CNN variant comparison, and it does not discuss failure cases or limitations. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and hyperparameters are provided, but code availability is not mentioned, which weakens reproducibility. From an applied-systems perspective this is a practically useful and empirically-supported contribution with some minor gaps in analysis and transparency.

main_claims: Proposes a two-step supervised framework for identifying event factuality in raw texts.
Introduces an attention-based CNN to detect Source Introducing Predicates (SIPs).
Proposes an attention-based deep neural network combining BiLSTM and CNN to identify event factuality.
_summary.methodology: A pipeline that first extracts basic factors (SIPs, sources, cues) from raw text and then applies an attention-enhanced neural model that uses BiLSTM over shortest dependency paths and CNN over words (with auxiliary lexical features) to classify event factuality via two outputs (Uu vs Non-Uu and modality/polarity for Non-Uu).

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a PhD in computational linguistics and a track record of work on formal semantics and principled models of meaning.
traits: Downgrades papers that present engineering pipelines without formal or theoretical analysis of the model (e.g., no guarantees, no identifiability, no theoretical motivation): -2 to novelty/technical soundness scores
Penalises papers that do not quantify or model error propagation in multi-stage pipelines (expects explicit analysis or mitigation): -1 to empirical validity score if absent
Requires strong ablation studies isolating contribution of each component (will subtract points if <3 targeted ablations): -1 to clarity/experimental rigor
Prefers mechanistic/interpretable models and will deduct points for complex black-box architectures unless accompanied by probing/interpretability analyses: -1 to impact if absent
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on theoretical motivation, clear causal claims, and rigorous analysis of model components; I am skeptical of empirical-only contributions lacking formal grounding.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Systems Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Industry-affiliated researcher specializing in applied NLP, with experience building production pipelines for information extraction and event understanding.
traits: Rewards practical, end-to-end pipeline designs that enable use on raw text and emphasize replicability/engineering practicality: +2 to novelty/impact when present
Favors papers that introduce or improve task-specific architectures and demonstrate clear empirical gains on realistic benchmarks: +1 to empirical validity if gains are consistent

Values thorough experimental comparisons and error analyses that show real-world applicability (will penalize if no dataset/benchmark evaluations are provided): -1 to evaluation score if datasets/benchmarks missing or weak
Positively biases toward attention mechanisms and hybrid BiLSTM/CNN models that improve performance and are computationally feasible for deployment: +1 to technical soundness
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I evaluate papers by their practical utility, empirical improvements on benchmarks, and how readily their approaches can be adopted in real systems; novel engineering that shows consistent gains is highly valued.
